verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As to your charge of perjury, since you refer me to your book; and since I have made my reply to you and Calpurnius in the previous books, it will be sufficient here to observe that you exact from me in my sleep what you have never yourself fulfilled in your waking hours.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It seems that I am guilty of a great crime because I have told girls and virgins of Christ, that they had better not read secular works, and that I once promised when warned in a dream not to read them.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But your ship which was announced by revelation to the city of Rome, promises one thing and effects another.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It came to do away with the puzzle of the mathematici: what it does is to do away with the faith of Christians.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It had made its run with sails full set over the Ionian and Ægean, the Adriatic and Tyrrhenian seas, only to make shipwreck in the Roman port.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Are you not ashamed of hunting up nonsense of this kind and putting me to the trouble of bringing up similar things against you?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Suppose that some one had seen a dream about you such as might make you vainglorious; it would have been modest as well as wise in you not to seem to know of it, instead of boasting of other people’s dreams as a serious testimony to yourself.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What a difference there is between your dream and mine!

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Mine tells how I was humbled and repressed; yours boasts over and over again how you were praised.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You cannot say, It matters nothing to me what another man dreamed, for in those most enlightening books of yours you tell us that this was the motive which led you to make the translation; you could not bear that an eminent man should have dreamed in vain.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If you can make me out guilty of perjury, you think you will be deemed no heretic.
